name: Azure Synapse Analytics Architecture
description: Understanding the architecture of Azure Synapse Analytics including dedicated SQL pools, serverless SQL pools, Spark pools, and Synapse Link.
key_facts: Synapse Analytics integrates data warehousing (dedicated SQL pool), big data (Spark), and data integration (Synapse Pipelines) in one service
Dedicated SQL pool uses Massively Parallel Processing (MPP) with a Control node and Compute nodes
Serverless SQL pool is pay-per-query with no infrastructure to manage; data stays in the lake
Synapse Link enables near real-time analytics over operational data in Cosmos DB, SQL Server, and Dataverse without ETL
Distribution types for dedicated pool tables: Hash, Round-Robin, and Replicated; hash on high-cardinality join columns is best for large fact tables
common_misconceptions: Teams assume serverless SQL pool is slower than dedicated SQL pool for all queries—for ad-hoc exploration serverless can be faster as there is no warm-up time
Many believe Synapse Pipelines is just a rebranded Azure Data Factory—while sharing the same engine, Synapse Pipelines integrates directly with Synapse workspace artifacts

name: Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 Design
description: Designing and implementing Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 including hierarchical namespace, access control, and medallion architecture.
key_facts: ADLS Gen2 is built on Azure Blob Storage with hierarchical namespace (HNS) enabled for directory operations
Medallion architecture organizes data in Bronze (raw), Silver (cleaned), and Gold (aggregated/business-ready) layers
Access control supports both RBAC (coarse-grained, resource level) and POSIX-style ACLs (fine-grained, file/directory level)
Lifecycle management policies automatically tier or delete blobs based on last-modified or last-accessed date
Zone-Redundant Storage (ZRS) replicates data synchronously across three availability zones in the same region
common_misconceptions: Developers assume ADLS Gen2 ACLs override RBAC—both apply, and the most restrictive permission wins; RBAC Owner always has full access
Many believe enabling HNS on an existing Blob Storage account is reversible—HNS migration is a one-way process and cannot be undone

name: Azure Data Factory Pipelines and Data Flows
description: Designing and implementing data ingestion and transformation pipelines using Azure Data Factory, including Mapping Data Flows and Integration Runtimes.
key_facts: ADF pipelines orchestrate data movement and transformation using Activities (Copy, Data Flow, Execute Pipeline, etc.)
Integration Runtimes (IR) are the compute infrastructure: Azure IR (cloud), Self-hosted IR (on-premises), and Azure-SSIS IR (SSIS packages)
Mapping Data Flows are visually designed transformations that compile to Spark and run on ADF-managed Spark clusters
Triggers can be schedule-based, tumbling window, storage event, or custom event-based
Copy Activity supports 90+ source and sink connectors; parallel copy degree and staged copy via Blob improve throughput
common_misconceptions: Teams assume Mapping Data Flows and Wrangling Data Flows are interchangeable—Mapping Data Flows are code-generated Spark; Wrangling Data Flows use Power Query M language and are better for non-developers
Many believe Self-hosted IR can be installed on any machine—it requires Windows and specific outbound network connectivity to ADF control plane endpoints

name: Azure Databricks for Data Engineering
description: Using Azure Databricks clusters, notebooks, Delta Lake, and jobs for large-scale data processing and transformation.
key_facts: Azure Databricks is a managed Apache Spark platform optimized for Azure with AAD integration and VNet injection
Cluster types: All-purpose (interactive development) and Job clusters (automated pipelines, lower cost, auto-terminate)
Delta Lake adds ACID transactions, schema enforcement, time travel (versioning), and DML operations to Parquet files
Delta Live Tables (DLT) is a declarative framework for building reliable, maintainable, and testable data pipelines
Unity Catalog provides centralized governance for data and AI assets across multiple Databricks workspaces
common_misconceptions: Teams assume Azure Databricks and Synapse Spark pools are equivalent—Databricks has more advanced ML features (MLflow, Feature Store) while Synapse integrates more natively with the Azure ecosystem
Many believe Delta Lake requires Azure Databricks—Delta Lake is open source and can be used with any Spark environment including Synapse Spark

name: Spark Core Concepts for Data Engineering
description: Understanding Spark RDDs, DataFrames, Datasets, partitioning, caching, and performance optimization for data engineering workloads.
key_facts: DataFrames are the primary abstraction in modern PySpark; they are distributed collections with a schema
Lazy evaluation defers computation until an action (collect, show, write) is called, enabling optimization
Partitioning determines parallelism; repartition() causes a full shuffle while coalesce() reduces partitions without shuffle
Broadcast joins send the smaller table to all executors, eliminating shuffle for large-small table joins
Tungsten engine uses binary encoding and code generation to minimize JVM overhead and improve CPU efficiency
common_misconceptions: Developers assume more partitions always means faster processing—too many small partitions create scheduling overhead that can hurt performance
Many believe caching is always beneficial—caching large DataFrames that are only used once wastes memory and can cause eviction of other cached data

name: Azure Stream Analytics Real-Time Processing
description: Processing real-time streaming data using Azure Stream Analytics with Event Hub or IoT Hub as inputs and SQL-like query language.
key_facts: Stream Analytics uses a SQL-like query language (SAQL) to filter, aggregate, and join streaming data
Windowing functions: Tumbling (non-overlapping), Hopping (overlapping), Sliding (event-driven), Session (activity-based)
Late arrival tolerance and out-of-order tolerance settings control how late events are handled
Streaming Units (SUs) represent the compute resources; 6 SUs process roughly 1 MB/s throughput
Reference data joins enrich stream data with static or slowly-changing data from Blob Storage or SQL Database
common_misconceptions: Teams assume Stream Analytics replaces Event Hubs—Event Hubs is the ingestion layer while Stream Analytics is the processing layer; they work together
Many believe Stream Analytics output is always near real-time—aggregations over long windows introduce latency equal to the window duration

name: Azure Event Hubs and Kafka Integration
description: Ingesting high-throughput streaming data using Azure Event Hubs, including partitions, consumer groups, and Kafka compatibility.
key_facts: Event Hubs is a fully managed, real-time data ingestion service that can handle millions of events per second
Partitions enable parallel consumers; partition count cannot be decreased after creation and is set at namespace creation
Consumer groups allow multiple applications to independently read the same event stream at different offsets
Event Hubs for Kafka exposes a Kafka-compatible endpoint so Kafka producers/consumers work without code changes
Capture automatically archives events to Azure Blob Storage or ADLS Gen2 in Avro format
common_misconceptions: Teams assume Event Hubs and Service Bus are interchangeable—Event Hubs is for streaming/telemetry (pull model) while Service Bus is for transactional messaging (push/pull with competing consumers)
Many believe adding more consumer groups improves throughput—consumer groups provide independent read positions but do not increase partition throughput

name: Synapse Dedicated SQL Pool Performance Optimization
description: Optimizing query performance in Synapse dedicated SQL pools using distribution strategies, indexes, partitioning, and workload management.
key_facts: Data Warehouse Units (DWUs) scale compute independently from storage; pausing the pool stops compute charges
Clustered Columnstore Index (CCI) is the default and best index for fact tables; requires good row group quality (1M+ rows per group)
Table partitioning improves query performance by partition elimination; partition on date columns for time-series data
Result-set caching caches query results for repeated identical queries; cache is invalidated when underlying data changes
Workload management uses workload groups and classifiers to allocate resources and prevent query queuing
common_misconceptions: Teams assume scaling DWUs linearly improves all queries—complex queries with data skew may not scale linearly
Many use hash distribution on all large tables—tables joined to a hash-distributed table should be distributed on the join key to avoid data movement

name: Data Security and Privacy in Azure Data Services
description: Implementing security controls including encryption, dynamic data masking, row-level security, and network isolation for Azure data services.
key_facts: Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) encrypts data at rest using AES-256; enabled by default in Azure SQL and Synapse
Dynamic Data Masking (DDM) obfuscates sensitive columns for non-privileged users without changing stored data
Row-Level Security (RLS) uses filter predicates to restrict row access based on user identity or group membership
Always Encrypted protects sensitive data by encrypting at the client driver; server never sees plaintext
Private Endpoints ensure data service traffic stays on the Microsoft backbone network and never traverses the public internet
common_misconceptions: Teams assume DDM prevents privileged users from seeing data—users with db_datareader or higher roles can bypass DDM
Many believe TDE with customer-managed keys (CMK) is automatically more secure—CMK shifts responsibility to the customer who must manage key rotation and availability

name: Monitoring and Optimizing Data Pipelines
description: Monitoring data pipelines and jobs using Azure Monitor, Synapse Monitor, Log Analytics, and optimizing for performance and cost.
key_facts: Synapse Monitor hub provides a unified view of pipeline runs, Spark job runs, SQL requests, and integration runtimes
Azure Monitor Diagnostic settings send ADF/Synapse logs and metrics to Log Analytics, Storage, or Event Hubs
Activity Runs and Pipeline Runs logs in ADF include input, output, and duration for each activity execution
Databricks jobs expose metrics via Ganglia UI and can be forwarded to Azure Monitor using the Log Analytics agent
Cost optimization for Synapse Spark: use auto-scaling clusters, choose appropriate node types, and terminate idle clusters
common_misconceptions: Teams assume pipeline failures always trigger email alerts by default—alerts must be explicitly configured in Azure Monitor or ADF trigger alert rules
Many believe monitoring logs are retained indefinitely—Log Analytics retention defaults to 30 days and must be explicitly extended

name: Azure Cosmos DB Analytical Store and Synapse Link
description: Enabling and using Cosmos DB Analytical Store with Synapse Link for no-ETL analytics over operational data.
key_facts: Analytical Store is a columnar store automatically synced from the Cosmos DB transactional store with no impact on RUs
Synapse Link connects Synapse Analytics directly to the Cosmos DB Analytical Store for serverless SQL or Spark analysis
Schema representation can be 'Well-defined schema' (uniform documents) or 'Full fidelity schema' (dynamic/varied documents)
The Time-To-Live (TTL) for Analytical Store can be set independently from the transactional store TTL
Synapse Link for Dataverse enables analytics over Microsoft 365 and Power Platform data using the same mechanism
common_misconceptions: Teams assume Synapse Link requires data to be copied to the data lake—Synapse Link queries the Cosmos DB Analytical Store directly without data movement
Many believe Analytical Store queries consume Cosmos DB RUs—Analytical Store operations are billed separately and do not consume transactional RUs

name: Synapse Serverless SQL Pool Querying
description: Querying data lake files using Synapse serverless SQL pool with OPENROWSET, external tables, and views over Parquet, CSV, and Delta Lake.
key_facts: OPENROWSET is the T-SQL function for querying files in ADLS Gen2 directly without loading data
External tables provide a reusable schema definition over files; data stays in the lake
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE AS SELECT (CETAS) writes query results to Parquet or CSV files in the data lake
Serverless SQL pool supports Parquet, CSV, JSON, and Delta Lake (with DELTA() provider function) formats
Pricing is $5 per TB of data processed; query optimization via column pruning and predicate pushdown reduces cost
common_misconceptions: Teams assume serverless SQL pool can write data back to existing tables—CETAS creates new external files; DML operations are not supported
Many believe all SQL Server T-SQL syntax works in serverless pool—it supports a subset of T-SQL and lacks features like temp tables created with SELECT INTO

name: Data Warehouse Modeling Patterns
description: Designing star schema, snowflake schema, and slowly changing dimensions for analytical data warehouse workloads.
key_facts: Star schema has fact tables at center connected to denormalized dimension tables; optimized for query performance
Snowflake schema normalizes dimension tables into sub-dimensions; saves storage but requires more joins
Slowly Changing Dimension (SCD) Type 1 overwrites old values, Type 2 adds new rows with effective dates, Type 3 adds columns
Surrogate keys (integer) are preferred over natural/business keys in dimension tables for join performance
Fact tables should be narrow (few columns) with many rows; dimension tables wider with descriptive attributes
common_misconceptions: Teams assume snowflake schema is always better because it's normalized—in data warehousing, denormalization (star schema) improves analytical query performance
Many use natural keys from source systems as dimension keys—natural keys change over time in source systems; surrogate keys provide stability

name: Azure Data Factory Mapping Data Flows Transformations
description: Implementing complex data transformations using ADF Mapping Data Flows with aggregate, join, pivot, window, and conditional split transformations.
key_facts: Mapping Data Flows are visually designed and compiled to Spark; no Spark coding required
Transformations: Source, Sink, Join, Lookup, Aggregate, Derived Column, Filter, Select, Pivot, Unpivot, Window
Data flow debug mode spins up a Spark cluster for interactive row sampling and testing
The Conditional Split transformation routes rows to different streams based on filter conditions (like WHERE/CASE)
Parameterizing data flows enables reuse across different sources/sinks by passing values at pipeline runtime
common_misconceptions: Teams assume Mapping Data Flows are always faster than Copy Activity for simple moves—for straight file copy without transformation, Copy Activity is more efficient and cheaper
Many believe Data Flow debug clusters are free—debug mode spins up a Spark cluster billed at the standard ADF Data Flow cluster rate

name: PolyBase and COPY INTO for Data Loading
description: Loading large volumes of data into Synapse dedicated SQL pool using PolyBase external tables and the COPY INTO command.
key_facts: COPY INTO is the recommended method for bulk loading into Synapse; simpler syntax and no external objects needed
PolyBase requires creating External Data Source, External File Format, and External Table before loading
Best practice: load into a staging table first, then merge into the production table to minimize locking
Supported formats for COPY INTO: Parquet, ORC, CSV/delimited text
Loading data in parallel using multiple files that match partition boundaries dramatically increases throughput
common_misconceptions: Teams assume PolyBase and COPY INTO have identical performance—COPY INTO is generally faster and simpler for most scenarios
Many try to load directly into CCI tables in real time—CCIs work best for bulk loads; use heap or clustered rowstore for frequent small inserts
